summary: Azure Firewall is a fully stateful, cloud-native network firewall service with built-in high availability and unlimited scalability that protects Azure workloads. It inspects both east-west (internal) and north-south (inbound/outbound) traffic. It comes in three SKUs — Basic, Standard, and Premium — each offering increasing levels of threat protection.

key_concepts.explanation: Entry-level SKU for SMBs; supports Threat Intel in alert-only mode, fixed 2-VM backend, recommended up to 250 Mbps throughput.
Provides L3-L7 filtering with real-time threat intelligence from Microsoft Cyber Security, blocking known malicious IPs and domains.
Adds signature-based IDPS with 67,000+ signatures across 50+ categories for detecting malware, phishing, coin mining, and Trojans.
Centralized management plane for deploying and governing Azure Firewalls across multiple subscriptions using shared firewall policies.
Tracks the state of network connections, meaning return traffic is automatically allowed without explicit rules — unlike stateless packet filtering.
East-west is traffic between internal resources; north-south is traffic between the internet and internal resources. Azure Firewall inspects both.

exam_focus_points: Azure Firewall is a fully stateful firewall as a service — distinguish from NSGs which are stateless packet filters
Three SKUs: Basic (SMB, alert-only Threat Intel, 250 Mbps), Standard (L3-L7 + Threat Intel block mode), Premium (IDPS with 67,000+ signatures)
Azure Firewall Premium is the only SKU with signature-based IDPS — required for detecting malware and exploit patterns
Azure Firewall Manager is used for centralized, multi-subscription firewall policy management
Azure Firewall supports both Virtual Network and Virtual WAN (Secure Virtual Hub) deployments
Azure Firewall is in the Network Security category alongside Azure DDoS Protection and Azure Web Application Firewall (WAF)
similar_concepts: Azure Front Door with WAF policy
common_mistakes: Confusing Azure Firewall with NSGs — NSGs are stateless L3/L4 controls on subnets/NICs; Azure Firewall is a stateful, centralized L3-L7 appliance
Assuming Basic SKU supports Threat Intel block mode — Basic only supports alert mode, not deny/block
Thinking Azure Firewall Premium is just Standard with more throughput — the key differentiator is IDPS (intrusion detection and prevention), not performance
Confusing Azure Firewall Manager with Azure Monitor — Manager is for policy/governance of firewalls, not for metrics or diagnostics
Assuming Azure Firewall alone protects against DDoS or web app attacks — WAF and DDoS Protection are separate services for those threat vectors
